# Extended Case References — Lecture JqHyPQAPsao

*Brief mentions of canonical cases that did not warrant a full `case_index.md` entry. Generated by reconciliation pass against the 1,676-cluster canon. Substantive treatments are in `case_index.md`.*

## Cases mentioned in passing

### West Grafton forging press I-beam crack
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "West Grafton forging press I-beam crack"
- **Anchor:** `§3.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "With that I have one last thing which sort of relates to you. It's another forging press problem, which I think I can do fairly quickly. The reason it's interesting is because it shows you what happens and how we lost our technology to weld very thick steel. Back in the 1950s when there was lots of money to protect ourselves against the Soviet threat, the Air Force was trying to get rid of welding in their aircraft, so they wanted bigger and bigger forging presses. They paid to build two 50,000-ton forging presses. These were the two largest forging presses anywhere in what we call the free..."

### West Grafton forging press I-beam crack
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "West Grafton forging press I-beam crack"
- **Anchor:** `§3.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "In the early '80s the one in West Grafton developed a crack in one of the six support beams. Now this is the world's largest forging press. Above ground it's got a big frame up here, but you can't make it out of a solid aluminum casting. It stands maybe 80 feet above ground, and then it goes about 60 feet below ground. You actually had some I-beams down here — six I-beams if I remember. The I-beams were about ten feet tall, the web was 10 foot, the thickness of the web was 10 inches, and the flanges if I remember were like 15 inches thick. They had six of these going across instead of a great..."
